name: HP Inc.

profile.description: HP Inc. is a global provider of personal computing, printing, 3D printing, hybrid work, and gaming technology. The company operates through three segments: Personal Systems (71% of revenue, including commercial/consumer desktops, notebooks, workstations, and endpoint security), Printing (commercial and consumer printers, supplies, and 3D printing solutions), and Corporate Investments. Founded as Hewlett-Packard in 1939, HP Inc. was spun off from Hewlett Packard Enterprise in 2015 and is headquartered in Palo Alto, California.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): HP's most significant transformation was the 2015 split from Hewlett Packard Enterprise, separating the PC/printing business from IT services. Since then, HP has launched a meaningful AI PC portfolio — EliteBook, Z workstations with on-device AI, and the HP IQ platform announced in March 2026 — representing a genuine strategic pivot from commodity hardware vendor toward software-orchestrated enterprise platform [1][2]. However, the core printing business continues to shrink structurally, and no business model transformation of iPhone scale has occurred. AI Deployment (1/2): HP IQ (on-device intelligence platform) is shipping across commercial PC lines, and AI-enhanced features are live in the EliteBook and Z workstation portfolio [2]. However, no specific AI-driven revenue figures or quantified cost savings have been disclosed. Market Share (1/2): HP is the #2 global PC vendor behind Lenovo with meaningful share, and maintains dominant positions in certain enterprise printing categories. Personal Systems segment grew 13% in Q2 2026 [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): HP IQ platform announced with specific enterprise integrations, AI PC roadmap extends through 2026-2027 with named products. 3D printing expansion in commercial/industrial segments has specific customer wins [2]. R&D Intensity (0/1): HP's R&D spend as a percentage of revenue is below the technology sector average for a hardware company with commodity margins. Gross margin of 20.3% limits R&D reinvestment capacity [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): HP's stated purpose — 'technology that makes life better for everyone, everywhere' — is specific, aspiring, and has historical grounding in the legendary HP Way: Bill Hewlett and Dave Packard's garage startup ethos that shaped Silicon Valley's culture of innovation with purpose [4]. The purpose has survived 86 years and the 2015 company split. However, the degree to which it shapes modern HP Inc.'s strategic decisions is unclear given ongoing restructurings and financial engineering. Community Impact (1/2): HP has the HP Foundation, STEM education initiatives, circular economy programs, and employee volunteering with measurable reach. However, no specific founding-level dollar amounts or distinctive program data were identified that would warrant a 2 [4]. Employee Retention (0/2): CEO Enrique Lores stepped down as President and CEO effective February 3, 2026 — a significant leadership disruption — with Bruce Broussard appointed as Interim CEO [5]. HP has also executed multiple rounds of workforce reductions, with 8,000+ jobs eliminated in recent restructuring cycles. These signals reflect organizational instability rather than a high-trust environment. Insider Buying (0/2): CEO Lores made 12 insider transactions over 5 years — all sales, zero purchases — including $1.09M in shares sold in December 2025 and additional sales in September 2025, both under Rule 10b5-1 plans [5]. Pure selling from the top executive is a conviction-absent signal.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available dividend-stock metrics. Liquidity passes: avg daily volume ~18.4M shares x $25.49 = ~$469M daily trading value [3]. D/E and ROE excluded (negative book value from aggressive share buybacks creates undefined/negative equity — a balance sheet artifact rather than insolvency signal; total debt $10.9B vs market cap $23.4B and $2.8B FCF) [3]. Revenue growth YoY of 6.9% (Q1 FY2026) scores 0.90 — meaningful growth but below 10% target [1]. Revenue growth 5yr CAGR of ~0% scores 0.33 — HP has been largely flat over five years reflecting a mature hardware market. Forward P/E of 8.6 scores 1.0 — attractively valued. Profit margin of 4.5% scores 0.22 — thin hardware margins are a structural feature. Dividend metrics are strong: payout ratio 44.3% scores 0.93, 11 consecutive years paid scores 1.0, 11 consecutive years growth scores 1.0, 5yr dividend CAGR of ~6.5% scores 1.0 [4]. Weighted sum: (1x0.90 + 1x0.33 + 1x1.0 + 1x0.22 + 1x0.93 + 2x1.0 + 1x1.0 + 2x1.0) / 10 x 10 = 8.4 -> 8.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (5/9): HP faces a bifurcated AI risk profile across its two segments. The Printing segment is structurally EXPOSED — AI-driven digital document workflows, reduced paper consumption, and enterprise automation directly suppress print demand. Management has guided for the entire print market to decline by a low single-digit percentage annually in both 2025 and 2026, and printing hardware units fell 12% in Q4 2025 [2]. The Personal Systems segment is more PROTECTED — AI is actually a PC refresh catalyst (the Windows 11/AI PC upgrade cycle), and HP's new AI PCs with on-device HP IQ processing represent hardware that benefits from AI adoption. However, HP sells commodity hardware in a highly competitive market, and the long-term risk of AI reducing the need for physical computing devices cannot be dismissed. AI Opportunity (1/1): HP IQ platform with on-device AI intelligence is shipping across commercial PC lines (EliteBook, Z workstations), and HP has announced specific AI-enabled products for the enterprise. This qualifies as AI products shipping to customers even though AI-specific revenue is not yet quantified [2].

infinite_game.summary: HP Inc. scores as a FINITE player. The company carries Hewlett-Packard's storied legacy and a genuine foundational purpose, but modern HP Inc. has been characterized by financial engineering (debt-funded buybacks, negative equity), serial restructurings, CEO turnover, and workforce reductions rather than the kind of long-term mission-first decisions that Sinek would recognize. The 2015 HPE split showed some strategic boldness, but the subsequent years have been more about managing a declining print business than playing an infinite game.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): HP's purpose — 'technology that makes life better for everyone, everywhere' — is grounded in the authentic HP Way: the garage founding story, the principle of treating employees with respect, and Silicon Valley's original culture of invention for human benefit [4]. The cause has genuine historical roots in Hewlett and Packard's philosophy that predates modern corporate purpose marketing. However, modern HP Inc. has been through three CEOs, multiple major restructurings, and aggressive financial engineering that often prioritizes quarterly returns over long-term mission. The cause is real but not consistently lived in the current era.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): CEO Enrique Lores departed effective February 3, 2026 after 6 years in the role, with Interim CEO Bruce Broussard stepping in [5]. HP has also executed multiple rounds of significant workforce reductions — over 8,000 jobs in recent restructuring cycles. These signals are the opposite of what Sinek describes as a Circle of Safety. High-profile leadership changes and layoff cycles erode the psychological safety that enables people to take risks and advance the company's cause.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): HP's competitive language is primarily focused on winning market share against Lenovo and Dell in PC hardware, and managing decline in the print market. Management commentary centers on hardware differentiation and margin management rather than using competitors' strengths as a mirror for self-improvement. No evidence of Sinek-style 'worthy rival' framing in HP's investor or strategic communications [1][2].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The 2015 separation from Hewlett Packard Enterprise was a meaningful strategic act — creating two focused companies rather than one sprawling conglomerate [4]. HP Inc. has also pivoted into 3D printing (an entirely new manufacturing category) and is repositioning from hardware vendor to software-orchestrated platform with HP IQ. These are incremental evolutions, not the transformative bet that Sinek exemplifies with Apple's Mac-to-iPhone move, but they demonstrate meaningful flexibility for a mature hardware company.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): The HPE split required conviction — splitting a 76-year-old company in two is not an easy decision, and both HP Inc. and HPE have performed better as focused entities than the combined Hewlett-Packard likely would have [4]. The 3D printing bet in industrial markets requires sustained R&D investment with uncertain returns. However, HP's financial engineering — debt-funded share buybacks that have driven equity negative — suggests capital allocation prioritizes stock price over long-term capability building. Sinek would see the buyback strategy as finite-game optimizing.

investment_thesis: HP Inc. is a capital-efficient cash-flow machine trading at 8.6x forward earnings with a 4.7% dividend yield — deeply discounted to the S&P 500 and even hardware peers [3]. Q2 FY2026 delivered 9% revenue growth (eighth consecutive quarter of positive growth) with Personal Systems up 13%, suggesting the AI PC refresh cycle is underway [1]. Free cash flow of $2.8B in FY2025 supports both the $1.20/share annual dividend (payout ratio 44%) and an active share buyback program ($325M in Q1 FY2026 alone) [3][4]. At sub-10x earnings with dividend growth for 11 consecutive years, the stock screens as a value/income opportunity for IOWN's dividend sleeve despite secular headwinds.
thesis_continued: The bear case is structural: HP is a hardware vendor in two declining or commodity markets. The print segment faces persistent structural decline as digital workflows reduce paper consumption — management guides for the print market to shrink at a low single-digit rate annually [2]. The PC market is cyclical and highly competitive; Personal Systems gross margins are thin and subject to component cost volatility. CEO Lores' February 2026 departure introduces leadership uncertainty during a critical AI PC positioning moment, and the Interim CEO from a healthcare background is an unusual choice for a technology company [5]. The stock trades cheaply because the market is pricing in stagnating earnings — HP's 5-year revenue CAGR is approximately zero, and EPS grew only modestly in FY2025 before declining in Q1 FY2026. The dividend is well-covered but growth is dependent on sustained PC demand.
key_catalysts: AI PC refresh cycle acceleration: Windows 11 end-of-support in October 2025 and AI PC enterprise refresh could drive 2-3 years of above-trend PC demand, with HP's AI-native portfolio (HP IQ, EliteBook AI) positioned to capture commercial share

HP IQ platform monetization: If HP successfully converts from hardware vendor to subscription software platform (as signaled by the HP IQ launch), recurring software revenue could structurally improve margins and justify multiple expansion

Permanent CEO appointment with a credible enterprise technology background to replace Interim CEO Broussard could restore institutional investor confidence and provide strategic clarity on the AI PC/printing pivot
key_risks: Structural print market decline accelerating: Management guidance of low single-digit annual print market contraction could worsen as enterprise AI adoption reduces document printing — the print segment is approximately 29% of revenue and generates higher margins than PCs

CEO vacuum at a critical inflection point: Enrique Lores' departure in February 2026 during the AI PC transformation creates strategic uncertainty; Interim CEO Bruce Broussard has no prior technology hardware experience

Margin compression from component costs and competition: HP's 4.5% net profit margin leaves minimal buffer against DRAM/NAND price spikes, tariff exposure on China-assembled products, or pricing pressure from Lenovo and Dell in the PC market
sources: HP Inc. Q2 FY2026 Earnings Release — May 27, 2026 (HP Investor Relations / GlobeNewswire)
